coredata.dc:title: Prominent enhancement of stability under high current density of LiFePO4-based multidimensional nanocarbon composite as cathode for lithium-ion batteries
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Kim, Jihyun
Song, Seunghyun
Lee, Churl Seung
Lee, Minbaek
Bae, Joonho
coredata.dc:description: A facile method for synthesizing carbon-coated lithium iron phosphate (LiFePO4, LFP) and an LFP-based multidimensional nanocarbon composite to enhance the electrochemical performance of lithium-ion batteries is presented herein. Three types of cathode materials are prepared: carbon-coated LFP (LC), carbon-coated LFP with carbon nanotubes (LC@C), and carbon-coated LFP with carbon nanotubes/graphene quantum dots (LC@CG). The electrochemical performances of the LC-nanocarbon composites are compared, and both LC@C and LC@CG show improved electrochemical performance than LC. Compared with both the LC and LC@C electrodes, the LC@CG electrode exhibits the highest specific capacity of 107.1 mA h g−1 under 20C of current density, as well as higher capacities and greater stability over all measured current densities. Moreover, after 300 charge–discharge cycles, the LC@CG electrode exhibits the best stability than the LC and LC@C electrodes. This is attributable to the graphene quantum dots, which enhance the morphological stability of the LC@CG electrode during electrochemical measurements. Our findings suggest that LFP-nanocarbon composites are promising as cathode materials and highlight the potential of graphene quantum dots for improving the stability of cathodes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium iron phosphate
Graphene quantum dots
Multidimensional nanocarbon composite
Cathode
Lithium-ion batteries

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Energy Conversion and Storage and Environmental Technologies

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of LC, LC@C, and LC@CG composites

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of synthesis processes of LC, LC@C, and LC@CG.
XRD patterns of standard LFP (PDF No. 01-080-6252), LC, LC@C, and LC@CG powder samples.

(a) Nitrogen adsorption and desorption isotherm curves of LC, LC@C, and LC@CG powder samples. (Inset table shows specific surface area values of the three samples.) (b) TGA curves of LC, LC@C, and LC@CG powder samples. (Inset table shows weight loss information of the three samples.)
TEM photographs of (a, d) LC, (b, e) LC@C, and (c, f) LC@CG powder samples at different magnifications.

(a) Rate capability of LC, LC@C, and LC@CG electrodes at various C-rates. (b) Polarization profiles of LC, LC@C, and LC@CG electrodes between charge and discharge plateaus at a specific capacity of 100 mA h g−1. (c) and (d) Voltage profiles of LC, LC@C, and LC@CG electrodes at current densities of 0.5C and 20C, respectively. (e) Cyclic stability of LC, LC@C, and LC@CG electrodes at 1C for 300 cycles.

(a) CV profiles of LC, LC@C, and LC@CG electrodes. (b) EIS spectra of LC, LC@C, and LC@CG electrodes with fitting results. (c) Equivalent circuit of LC (top), LC@C and LC@CG (bottom) for fitting experimental results. (d) Relationship between reciprocal square root of angular frequency and actual impedance in low-frequency region of LC, LC@C, and LC@CG electrodes.
Calculated lattice parameters of LC, LC@C, and LC@CG powder samples and volume values of unit cell obtained from XRD data.
RS, RCT, and DLi+ values of LC, LC@C, and LC@CG electrodes.

head.ce:title: Prominent enhancement of stability under high current density of LiFePO4-based multidimensional nanocarbon composite as cathode for lithium-ion batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nanoscience and Technology (Nano-physics), Gachon University, 1342 Seungnam-daero, Sujeong-gu, Sengnam-si, Gyeonggi-do 13120, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Nanoscience and Technology (Nano-physics), Gachon University, 1342 Seungnam-daero, Sujeong-gu, Sengnam-si, Gyeonggi-do 13120, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nano Convergence Technology Research Center, Korea Electronics Technology Institute, 25 Saenari-ro, Bundang-gu, Seongnam-si, Gyeonggi-do 13509, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nano Convergence Technology Research Center, Korea Electronics Technology Institute, 25 Saenari-ro, Bundang-gu, Seongnam-si, Gyeonggi-do 13509, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Inha University, 100 Inha-ro, Michuhol-gu, Incheon 22212, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Inha University, 100 Inha-ro, Michuhol-gu, Incheon 22212, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile method for synthesizing carbon-coated lithium iron phosphate (LiFePO4, LFP) and an LFP-based multidimensional nanocarbon composite to enhance the electrochemical performance of lithium-ion batteries is presented herein. Three types of cathode materials are prepared: carbon-coated LFP (LC), carbon-coated LFP with carbon nanotubes (LC@C), and carbon-coated LFP with carbon nanotubes/graphene quantum dots (LC@CG). The electrochemical performances of the LC-nanocarbon composites are compared, and both LC@C and LC@CG show improved electrochemical performance than LC. Compared with both the LC and LC@C electrodes, the LC@CG electrode exhibits the highest specific capacity of 107.1 mA h g−1 under 20C of current density, as well as higher capacities and greater stability over all measured current densities. Moreover, after 300 charge–discharge cycles, the LC@CG electrode exhibits the best stability than the LC and LC@C electrodes. This is attributable to the graphene quantum dots, which enhance the morphological stability of the LC@CG electrode during electrochemical measurements. Our findings suggest that LFP-nanocarbon composites are promising as cathode materials and highlight the potential of graphene quantum dots for improving the stability of cathodes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have become a widely used power source for portable electronic devices, electric vehicles, and hybrid electric vehicles [1] owing to their high energy density [2], large operating potential window [3], and lightweight design [4]. The cathode, which is composed of high-energy–density materials, is key to the performance of LIBs [5]. Among the various available cathode materials, lithium iron phosphate (LiFePO4, LFP) is widely used owing to its stability [6], low cost [5], and environmental friendliness [7]. However, the limited lithium-ion kinetic of LFP due to 1-dimensional (1D) lithium-ion diffusion hinders its utility in high-performance LIBs, despite its high theoretical specific capacity (170 mA h g−1) and stable voltage plateau (Li+/Li at 3.45 V) [8–10]. Various approaches have been proposed to overcome this issue, such as doping [11], reducing the particle size [12], coating with a carbonaceous material [13], and fabricating LFP composites with conductive agents [14]. The encapsulation or hybridization of LFP particles with conducting materials is a widely used method to promote improved conductive channels and avoid the agglomeration of LFP particles during thermal treatment, thereby forming a fine structure [15,16]. Coating a conducting layer on the surface of LFP can enhance the surface conductivity and improve the rate performance. For example, Lai et al. [17] successfully encapsulated LFP with a uniform N/P co-doped carbon layer using expired beer as the carbon source, which resulted in rapid electron transport in the entire cathode. Meanwhile, Chien et al. [18] developed a uniform and conductive three-dimensional (3D) structure by combining graphene oxide with a wrinkled morphology with LFP, which resulted in an interconnected pore structure in the LFP-carbon (LFP-C) composite. This 3D structure enabled fast electron and lithium-ion transport, thereby improving the electrochemical performance of the composite.

Carbonaceous materials are typically used to improve the electrochemical performances of LFP composites [4,19,20]. Significant improvements in the electrochemical performance of LFP-carbon composites have been demonstrated via the use of carbon materials of various dimensions [21–23]. Among the various carbonaceous materials, 1D multiwalled carbon nanotubes (CNTs) are of particular interest because of their high electrical conductivity [24] and large specific surface area [25]. However, CNTs are restricted when they are used to form a continuous network with LFP [26,27], which can result in practical issues associated with the high rate capability and cycling stability of the LFP–CNT composite. Low-dimensional carbon materials, such as graphene quantum dots (GQDs), can enhance the electrochemical performance of LFP-C electrode materials by facilitating connections between LFP particles and CNTs. GQDs feature a small particle size and high electrical conductivity [28], which render them ideal for creating efficient pathways to facilitate electron and lithium-ion transport in composites [29,30]. Incorporating GQDs into the LFP–CNT composite can address the limitations associated with the practical use of LFP–CNT composites, such as their low rate capabilities and cycling stabilities. Therefore, GQDs are a promising additive for LFP–CNT composites that can enhance the electrochemical performance of the composites in LIBs.

In this study, we synthesize a carbon-coated LFP–CNT–GQD composite (LC@CG) using a facile solvothermal technique, and evaluate its electrochemical performance. Specifically, a well-dispersed multidimensional nanocarbon composite comprising CNTs and GQDs is treated in a viscous solvent obtained via stirring and sonication. The prepared LC@CG exhibits outstanding electrochemical performance, surpassing those of both carbon-coated LFP (LC) and carbon-coated LFP–CNT composites (LC@C). Additionally, it offers an impressive specific capacity of 107.1 mA h g−1 at the highest current density of 20C (1C = 170 mA g−1), as well as an excellent capacity retention of 99.4 % after 300 cycles at a current density of 1C. The observed improvement in the electrochemical characteristics of the LFP–CNT composite upon the incorporation of zero-dimensional GQDs underscores the potential of LC@CG as a highly promising cathode material for enhancing the stability of LIBs.

Initially, 0.1 g of high-purity graphite (99.9 %, US Research Nanomaterials, Inc.) was dispersed in an acidic liquid composed of high-purity HNO3 (65.0 %, EMSURE®) and H2SO4 (98.0 %, Sigma Aldrich) at a ratio of 1:3, followed by sonication for 2 h. The resulting acidic liquid was magnetically stirred at 130 °C for 24 h and then cooled naturally to room temperature. Subsequently, a Na2CO3 (99.8 %, Fujifilm Wako Chemicals) aqueous solution was added gradually while maintaining the pH level at 8.0. Subsequently, the neutralized solution was filtered to eliminate impurities and then freeze-dried for three days.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of LC, LC@C, and LC@CG composites
ce:section.ce:para: The LC@CG nanocomposites were synthesized via a conventional solvothermal method. Initially, 14.4 mmol of LiOH·H2O (98.0 %, Sigma–Aldrich), 4.8 mmol of H3PO4 (85.0 %, Sigma–Aldrich), and 4.8 mmol of FeSO4·7H2O (99.9 %, Sigma Aldrich) were sequentially dissolved in high-purity ethylene glycol (99.0 %, Sigma–Aldrich) under vigorous stirring. Subsequently, 30 mg of CNTs (97.0 %, Nanochemazone®) and 15 mg of prepared GQDs were added to the green solution above and then stirred sonicated and magnetically to ensure a homogeneous dispersion before being transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. The resulting mixture was subjected to solvothermal treatment at 180 °C for 4 h. After cooling to room temperature, the precipitate was purified several times with ethylene alcohol and dried at 70 °C overnight in a vacuum oven. Subsequently, 15 wt % sucrose (99.5 %, Duchefa) as a carbon precursor was mixed with the obtained powder in a mortar and carbonated at 650 °C for 90 min in a quartz tube furnace under Ar gas flowing at a rate of 90 cc min−1. For comparison, LC and LC@C composites were synthesized under the same conditions, except that the LC@C composite was obtained without the addition of GQDs, and the LC synthesis procedure excluded both CNTs and GQDs (see Fig.1 ).

The materials used in this study were characterized using analytical techniques. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained utilizing an X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Smartlab, Japan) with Cu Kα source radiation. The carbon content of the samples was determined via thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) using a thermal analyzer system (Discovery SDT 650, TA Instrument, United States). The morphology and surface properties of the samples were investigated using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Titan G2 80–200, FEI Company, United States). The porosity of the samples was evaluated using a surface area and porosimetry analyzer system (ASAP 2020, Micromeritics, United States) by analyzing the nitrogen adsorption and desorption isotherms.

The electrochemical performance of the fabricated coin cells (CR2032) was evaluated. The cathodes were fabricated by a drawdown method using a slurry composed of the active material (LC, LC@C, and LC@GC), a conductive additive (carbon black), and a binder (polyvinylidene fluoride, PVDF) at a weight ratio of 8:1:1 mixed in a solvent (N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone) onto a current collector (aluminum foil). The coated substrate was subsequently heated at 90 °C for 12 h, and the coin cells were assembled in a glove box under an Ar atmosphere. Despite efforts to ensure consistency in the wet coating parameters using the slurry, variations in the loaded mass (1.02 ± 0.0970 mg) might have arisen due to the utilization of the hand drawdown method. Pure metallic lithium and Celgard® 2400 membrane, along with 1 M LiPF6 in a solution comprising ethylene carbonate, dimethyl carbonate, and ethyl methyl carbonate at a volume ratio of 1:1:1 were used as the counter electrode, separator, and electrolyte, respectively. The fabricated coin cells were tested galvanostatically at various current densities ranging from 0.5 to 20C using a CT2001A (Wuhan LAND Electronics Company, China) battery cycler system within a voltage window of 2.0–4.3 V. Furthermore, cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were performed on a Versastat 4 potentiostat (AMETEK, Inc., United Kingdom) within a voltage window of 2.0–4.3 V at a scan rate of 0.5 mV s−1 and a frequency range from 5 to 50 MHz.

Fig. 2 shows the XRD patterns of the standard LFP (PDF card no. 01-080-6252), LC, LC@C, and LC@CG powder samples. All the cathode materials corresponded well with the standard LFP peaks belonging to the space group Pnma of the orthorhombic structure. Owing to its low content, LC@CG exhibited a similar pattern without other secondary phases compared with LC and LC@C. The amorphous carbon coating and the addition of CNTs and GQDs did not destroy the intrinsic structure of LFP.

Table 1 lists the lattice parameters and unit cell volumes of the LC, LC@C, and LC@C powder samples obtained from the XRD results. The calculated lattice parameter and unit cell values were as follows: a = 10.35–10.37 Å, b = 6.02–6.47 Å, c = 4.53–4.71 Å, and V = 293.58–303.56 Å3. Whereas the LC exhibited the largest unit cell among the powder samples, the LC@C and LC@CG exhibited compressed volumes with shorter b-axis directions. Especially, the LC@CG was the closest to the lattice parameter values and unit cell volume in previous references [31,32]. The shortened b-axis pathway facilitated lithium-ion diffusion and thus might improve electrochemical performance [33].

Fig. 3 shows the BET and TGA profiles with inset tables that indicate the BET surface area and weight loss information of the three samples. The three samples exhibited mesoporous features, based on their typical type IV isotherm curves (see Fig. 3(a)) [34]. As shown in the inset of Fig. 3(a), LC@CG had the highest surface area of 44.58 m2 g−1 among the three samples. Meanwhile, LC and LC@C indicated lower surface area values of 21.29 and 37.45 m2 g−1, respectively. Based on Fig. 3(b), the three samples changed over two steps at 300 °C–400 °C and 400 °C–700 °C. In the range between 300 °C and 400 °C, the three samples gained weight due to the oxidation of Fe2+ in the LFP as following reaction (1) [35].
(1) LiFeⅡPO4 + 1/4O2 → 1/3Li3Fe2 Ⅲ(PO4)3 + 1/6Fe2 ⅢO3

At temperatures exceeding 400 °C, the weights decreased owing to the decomposition of carbon contents in their materials. As shown in the inset table of Fig. 3(b), the amounts of weight loss increased with the carbon content; the weight losses of LC, LC@C, and LC@CG were 1.44 %, 3.79 %, and 5.90 %, respectively.

Fig. 4 presents TEM images that show the surface morphology of the three samples. As shown in Fig. 4 (a) and (d), the LC is composed with LFP particles and amorphous carbon layers. In the LC@C images (Fig. 4 (b) and (e)), the appearance of CNTs was confirmed by LC. After the addition of GQDs to LC@C, GQDs structures were observed in LC@CG (Fig. 4 (c) and (f)). In all the three samples, the LFP particles were coated with a carbon layer, as shown in Fig. 4(d–f). The addition of sucrose serves as a carbon source, undergoing decomposition and carbonization during the sintering process. Forming amorphous carbon coating on the LFP particles provides additional electron carriers, thereby enhancing the electrical conductivity and electrochemical performance of the three samples. Although the GQDs could not be distinguished and verified readily in the SEM image of Fig. S3, the GQDs were clearly exposed on the surface of the carbon-coated LFP particles, as shown in Fig. 4(f). Surface reforming by adding GQDs is expected to effectively contribute to excellent electrochemical properties by increasing the movement of electrons and the transport of lithium ions under high currents.

Fig. 5 shows the specific capacities of the LC, LC@C, and LC@CG electrodes at various current densities from 0.5 to 20C. As shown in Fig. 5(a), the LC@C and LC@CG electrodes exhibited higher specific capacities than the LC electrode at all current densities. The LC electrode indicated average capacities of 148.0, 142.8, 139.1, 130.5, 123.8, 114.3, 100.78, and 88.1 mA h g−1 at current densities of 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 7, 10, 15, and 20C, respectively. The LC@C electrode indicated average capacities of 156.6, 152.7, 145.1, 131.6, 125.4, 118.1, 107.6, and 98.8 mA h g−1 at current densities of 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 7, 10, 15, and 20C, respectively. Compared with the LC and LC@C electrodes, the LC@CG electrode performed the best, with average capacities of 158.8, 154.7, 150.5, 138.5, 131.8, 124.5, 114.6, and 107.1 mA h g−1 at current densities of 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 7, 10, 15, and 20C, respectively. Additionally, when a current density of 0.5C was re-applied to the LC, LC@C, and LC@CG electrodes after a current density of 20C, the electrodes indicated average capacities of 145.3, 157.1, and 157.6 mA h g−1, respectively. The coulombic efficiencies of all three samples are also displayed in Fig. 5 (a), revealing predominantly high values in all rate capability tests. Temporary decreases were observed at the initial cycles for different C-rates, attributed to the increase in current density. In the initial cycles with high current density, LC@C and LC@CG show higher coulombic efficiencies compared to LC. It indicates that the carbon materials in prepared samples contribute to the improvement of cycling performance. All the cathodes returned to their initial capacities, indicating their satisfactory reversibility. As shown in Fig. 5(b), the charge and discharge voltage gaps and plateaus of the charge and discharge voltages became narrower and longer as the multidimensional carbon contents increased, respectively. Additionally, the differences in capacities were particularly significant as the current density increased in the LC and LC@C electrodes compared with the case of the LC@CG electrode. Although the specific capacities of the LC@C electrode improved compared with those of the LC electrode via the formation of networks between the CNTs and LC particles, a high current was applied to the LC@C electrode, which implies that the CNTs were likely to break the continuous networks with the LC particles by detaching [35]. Thus, by linking the CNTs and LFP to prevent detachment under high current densities, the GQDs in LC@CG improved the electrochemical performance. This suggests that introducing GQDs to LC@C can effectively support stable stability when the high current densities are applied to the battery.

Fig. 5(c, d) show the LC, LC@C, and LC@CG electrodes at current densities of 0.5 and 20C. The LC@CG electrode exhibited more stable plateaus by exploiting the most similar capacity compared to the theoretical capacity, as shown in Fig. 5(c). In addition, the polarizations between the charge and discharge voltage profiles of the LC, LC@C, and LC@CG electrodes, as shown in Fig. 5(b), were 62.1, 51.0, and 43.5 mV, respectively. This is particularly evident at the highest current density, as illustrated in Fig. 5(d). All cathodes exceeded a capacity of 80 mA h g−1 at the highest current density. In particular, the LC@CG electrode exhibited over a specific capacity of 107.2 mA h g−1, showed the most stable plateau region, and presented the narrowest polarization between the charge and discharge cycles. As predicted, the LC@CG electrode exhibited high lithium-ion diffusion and electron transport efficiencies owing to the addition of CNTs and GQDs.

A cyclic stability test was performed over 300 cycles at a constant current density of 1C, and the results are shown in Fig. 5(e). The first- and end-specific capacities of the LC, LC@C, and LC@CG electrodes were 143.7/141.4, 150.5/145.2, and 154.4/153.5 mA h g−1, respectively, and their capacity retentions were 98.4, 96.5, and 99.4 %, respectively. The LC electrode delivered relatively stable capacities, despite having the lowest capacities achieved over 300 cycles among the three samples. The cycling behavior of the LC@C electrode was unstable, although its specific capacity was higher than that of the LC electrode. The LC@CG electrode exhibited excellent cycling performance and maintained an ideal capacity retention.

Fig. 6 shows the CV and EIS profiles used to characterize the electrochemical behaviors of the LC, LC@C, and LC@CG cathodes. As shown in Fig. 6(a), all three samples exhibited two corresponding redox peaks in the potential range between 3.2 and 3.7 V. The sharpness and symmetry of the redox peaks of the LC@CG electrode were superior to those of the LC and LC@C electrodes. Furthermore, the LC@CG electrode indicated oxidation and reduction peaks that shifted to left and right, unlike the LC and LC@C electrodes. This smaller potential gap implies a lower polarization and higher capacity reversibility during charging and discharging compared with the case of the other two cathode materials. Fig. 6(b) shows the Nyquist plots of the LC, LC@C, and LC@CG electrodes in the discharged state, which allow us to further understand the lithium-ion kinetics of the three cathode samples. In the curves, the dots and lines represent the experimental and fitted data of the prepared sample electrodes, respectively. The first intercept of the semicircle on the Z'-axis in the high-frequency region corresponds to the resistance of the electrolyte, denoted as RS. The diameter of the semicircle on the Z'-axis in the high- to middle-frequency range represents the charge-transfer resistance, which is denoted as RCT. The tail-like line in the low-frequency region is related to the Warburg impedance, denoted as ZW, which affects lithium-ion diffusion in the electrode. The EIS curves were fitted using equivalent circuits, as shown in Fig. 6(c) [36,37]. The first circuit was used for fitting LC, and the other circuit was adopted to fit LC@C and LC@CG using the ZSimpwin software. As shown in the circuit, the resistance and capacitance components were added after the nanocarbon materials were introduced. The impedance values obtained from the analyses are listed in Table 2 . The RCT values exhibited a decreasing trend with increasing carbon content. The reduction in RCT is favorable for enhancing the electrochemical performance of the cathode by facilitating the kinetics of lithium ions during the charge and discharge processes. In the low-frequency region, the Warburg impedance coefficient (σw) was determined from the Z' vs. the reciprocal root square of angular frequency (ω−0.5) relationship using Equation (2). The corresponding curves are plotted in Fig. 6(d), which shows that LC@CG presents the gentlest slope. This indicates that the fastest lithium-ion diffusion occurred in LC@CG, based on the curves obtained from the reciprocal of the graph in the low-frequency range from the EIS results [38]. The lithium-ion diffusion coefficients (DLi+) of the three samples were calculated using Equation (2).
(2) Z' = RS + RCT + σwω−0.5
(3) DLi+ = R2T2 / 2A2F4n4C2σw 2

Here, DLi+ is the lithium-ion diffusion coefficient, R is the gas constant, T is the absolute temperature, A is the surface area of the electrode, F is Faraday’s constant, n is the number of electrons associated with the redox reaction, and C is the molar concentration of lithium ions. The Warburg coefficients shown in Fig. 6(b) and the calculated DLi+ values are listed in Table 2. Based on the calculation, all cathode samples indicated DLi+ values exceeding the theoretical value of LFP (10−13 to 10−16 cm2 s−1). Furthermore, compared with the LC and LC@C electrodes, the LC@CG electrode indicated the highest DLi+ value, which is attributable to enhancements in the conductivity and diffusivity at high current densities via the incorporation of GQDs.

A hierarchically structured LC@CG was developed for application as a cathode material in LIBs. The composite demonstrated significant improvements in the rate capability and cyclic stability compared with both LC and LC@C. The superior electrochemical performance of the abovementioned composite can be attributed to the synergistic effects of the amorphous carbon layers on the LFP particles and the networks between the LFP-embedded GQDs and CNTs, which facilitate the rapid transport of lithium ions and electrons at high current densities. This result shows the considerable enhancements in unstable electrochemical performance of CNTs containing electrode which is reported by another group. The findings suggest that the approach presented herein can be extended to other materials and customized accordingly to develop enhanced cathode materials for various applications in electrochemical energy storage. Our study represents a significant advancement in the development of LFP cathode materials for advanced LIBs.

Jihyun Kim: Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Validation, Conceptualization, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Seunghyun Song: Investigation, Validation, Visualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Churl Seung Lee: Formal analysis, Validation, Software. Minbaek Lee: Supervision, Resources. Joonho Bae: Supervision, Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Gachon University Research Fund of 2020 (GCU-202008480005). This study was supported by National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF-2023R1A2C1008272). This work was supported by the Institute of Information & Communications Technology Planning & Evaluation (IITP) grant funded by the Korean government (MSIT) (No. 2021-0-00259, Development of a Fast Wireless Charging System for Portable Terminals with improved heat dissipation and shielding performance).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2023.07.030.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
